subway_fire.title: Smoke-filled subway tunnel
subway_fire.description: You are inside a subway train stopped in a tunnel. Smoke is filling the carriage, visibility is low, and emergency doors are jammed.

viable_strategies.description: Stay low to avoid smoke inhalation.
Cover mouth and nose with cloth to filter smoke.
Follow the wall to maintain direction and avoid disorientation.
Actively search for maintenance or emergency exits.

subway_fire.difficulty_notes: Survival requires proactive movement and smoke mitigation. Passive behavior is fatal.

avalanche_burial.title: Buried under avalanche snow
avalanche_burial.description: You've been caught in an avalanche and are partially buried under dense, compacted snow. You have limited air supply and cannot see daylight. Your limbs are partially mobile.

viable_strategies.description: Cup hands over face immediately to create a breathing air pocket before snow sets.
Spit to determine which direction is down, then dig the opposite way.
Breathe slowly and avoid panicked movement to extend air supply.
Push compacted snow upward and outward in small deliberate motions.

common_failures: assuming rescuers will find you quickly
avalanche_burial.difficulty_notes: Snow sets like concrete within minutes. The air pocket creation window is extremely short.

capsized_boat.title: Capsized boat in open water
capsized_boat.description: Your small vessel has capsized in cold open water, miles from shore. The hull is floating inverted above you. You are treading water with no life jacket.

viable_strategies.description: Stay close to the inverted hull — it provides buoyancy and makes you visible to rescuers.
Limit movement to reduce heat loss and delay hypothermia onset.
Adopt HELP posture — draw knees to chest to protect core heat.
Use any reflective object, whistle, or arm signals to attract attention.

capsized_boat.difficulty_notes: Cold water kills faster than drowning. Swimming expends heat rapidly and is rarely survivable over distance.

desert_breakdown.title: Vehicle breakdown in remote desert
desert_breakdown.description: Your car has broken down on an unmaintained desert track. It is 42°C, you have half a bottle of water, and the nearest settlement is at least 40km away.

viable_strategies.description: Remain with the vehicle — it is far easier to spot from the air or road than a person on foot.
Rest in the shade of the car during peak heat hours to dramatically cut water loss.
Take small sips only — do not drink all water at once regardless of thirst.
Use mirrors, glass, or metallic surfaces to signal aircraft or distant vehicles.

common_failures: leaving the vehicle to find help
desert_breakdown.difficulty_notes: A walking human in extreme desert heat can die within hours. The vehicle is a survival asset, not a trap.

building_collapse.title: Trapped under collapsed building
building_collapse.description: An earthquake has caused a partial building collapse. You are trapped in a void space under rubble. You are injured but conscious. Dust is heavy in the air.

viable_strategies.description: Tap rhythmically on pipes or metal — it travels farther than voice and conserves energy.
Cover mouth and nose with fabric to avoid inhaling concrete dust.
Do not pull or shift surrounding debris, which can destabilize the void above you.
Stay still and calm to reduce oxygen consumption and manage injuries.

building_collapse.difficulty_notes: Secondary collapses kill many survivors. Patience and signaling discipline are critical.

riptide.title: Caught in a coastal riptide
riptide.description: You are swimming at a beach and have been pulled into a fast-moving riptide current. The shore is receding quickly. You are a moderate swimmer.

viable_strategies.description: Swim parallel to the shoreline to exit the narrow rip channel, not against it.
If exhausted, float on your back and conserve energy rather than fighting the current.
Wave one arm above your head — the universal distress signal for swimmers.
Once out of the rip channel, swim at a diagonal angle back toward shore using the waves.

riptide.difficulty_notes: Riptides are narrow but powerful. Fighting them head-on causes drowning by exhaustion in minutes.

lightning_storm_open_field.title: Caught in lightning storm in open terrain
lightning_storm_open_field.description: You are hiking on an exposed ridge when a fast-moving electrical storm rolls in. There is no shelter within 2km. Lightning is striking within 500 meters.

viable_strategies.description: Immediately descend from any elevated position — ridges and peaks attract strikes.
Crouch on the balls of your feet, feet together, hands over ears — minimizing ground contact and profile.
Stay away from isolated trees, poles, or rock formations that attract strikes.
If in a group, spread out at least 20m apart to avoid a single strike incapacitating everyone.

common_failures: sheltering under a tall isolated tree
lightning_storm_open_field.difficulty_notes: Lying flat maximizes ground current exposure. The crouch position is counterintuitive but correct.

house_fire_escape.title: Waking to a house fire at night
house_fire_escape.description: You wake at 2am to a smoke alarm. Your bedroom door is closed. The hallway outside is unknown. You are on the second floor with a window above a sloped roof.

viable_strategies.description: Touch the door with the back of your hand before opening — a hot door means fire is directly outside.
If opening the door, stay low and crawl — breathable air is within 30cm of the floor.
If door is hot, seal gaps with clothing to delay smoke entry and buy time.
Open window, hang from ledge to reduce drop height, then release to land and roll.

common_failures: opening a hot door without checking
jumping from window without hanging first
house_fire_escape.difficulty_notes: Toxic smoke renders people unconscious within 2-3 minutes. Speed and low posture are non-negotiable.

open_ocean_survival.title: Adrift in open ocean after shipwreck
open_ocean_survival.description: Your vessel has sunk. You are in a life raft with two others, no motor, limited supplies, and no confirmed rescue timeline. You are 200km from the nearest coast.

viable_strategies.description: Set up any available container or tarp to collect rainwater — the only safe hydration source.
Limit food to bare minimum — digestion accelerates dehydration in a salt environment.
Assign rotating watch shifts to continuously scan and signal any passing aircraft or vessels.
Cover all exposed skin and stay under any shade — sun exposure accelerates dehydration fatally.

common_failures: consuming all food in first 24 hours
open_ocean_survival.difficulty_notes: Drinking seawater accelerates death. The primary killers are dehydration, sun exposure, and despair-driven poor decisions.

thin_ice.title: Fallen through thin ice
thin_ice.description: You have broken through frozen lake ice. You are submerged to the chest in near-freezing water. The ice edge you broke through is within arm's reach.

viable_strategies.description: Use the initial cold shock adrenaline — your muscles will seize within 60-90 seconds, act immediately.
Kick legs to raise body horizontal, then claw and pull onto the ice surface in the direction you came from.
Once on the surface, roll — do not stand — to distribute weight and avoid re-breaking.
Crawl to shore without stopping — hypothermia will incapacitate you within minutes of exiting the water.

common_failures: trying to climb straight up on the ice edge
waiting for help instead of self-rescuing
standing up once on the ice
removing wet clothes before reaching shelter
thin_ice.difficulty_notes: Cold incapacitation is rapid and total. There is a narrow window of physical capability before muscles stop responding.

flash_flood_canyon.title: Flash flood in a slot canyon
flash_flood_canyon.description: You are hiking a narrow slot canyon when you hear a distant roar. Water begins rising around your ankles. The canyon walls are sheer with no obvious exit behind you.

viable_strategies.description: Climb the canyon wall using any available ledge, crack, or feature — water rises faster than you can run.
Abandon your pack and all heavy gear immediately — it will drag you under.
Target any ledge, pothole, or carved shelf above the waterline — even a meter of height can save you.
Do not run with the flood — debris and hydraulic forces in a channeled flood are lethal.

common_failures: attempting to swim through the flood
keeping heavy backpack on while climbing
waiting to see how high water rises
flash_flood_canyon.difficulty_notes: Flash floods in slot canyons move at car speed carrying boulders. Vertical escape is the only option.
